blocking_issues: The bundle mixes TCP/IP offload behavior with MMCSS scheduling behavior.

allowed_values.meaning: Microsoft documents that setting DisableTaskOffload to 0 enables task offloads.

allowed_values.label: Task offload disabled
allowed_values.meaning: Microsoft documents that setting DisableTaskOffload to 1 disables all task offloads from the TCP/IP transport.

allowed_values.label: App responsiveness value
allowed_values.meaning: Microsoft documents SystemResponsiveness as the percentage of CPU resources guaranteed to low-priority tasks. Values not divisible by 10 are rounded down to the nearest multiple of 10.

allowed_values.label: Primary-source mapping not yet confirmed
allowed_values.meaning: This review did not find a primary Microsoft source documenting the exact NetworkThrottlingIndex registry value.

records.name: Broad App Capability Deny Bundle

decision.why: Many underlying values are official, but the breadth and mixed-family nature of the bundle make it too opinionated for a simple validated toggle, and the policy surface should not be over-described as a complete Win32 privacy control.
blocking_issues: The bundle is very broad and mixes AppPrivacy policies with an extra System policy write.
The LetAppsAccess* policy family should not be presented as if it fully governs classic Win32 desktop apps.

records.name: Virtualization Based Security Policy Bundle
records.summary: Deprecated audit trail for the legacy Device Guard VBS bundle. The record documents the three official policy values, but the bundle is too broad and runtime-sensitive to publish as a single normalized control surface.

decision.why: This record was previously marked validated under schema 1.0 without machine-checkable validation proof and must be re-reviewed. The current app writes directly to the official Device Guard policy key and uses values that are explicitly documented in the local Microsoft policy files, but modern Windows 11 behavior, possible UEFI lock persistence, and Windows Hello dependencies mean registry proof alone is not enough to treat the bundle as a simple reversible disable switch.
blocking_issues: This legacy validated record did not store machine-checkable validation_proof and must be re-reviewed under schema 1.1.
Windows 11 24H2-class behavior can override or reassert effective VBS state after reboot even when policy values are written.
Windows Hello and PIN-backed scenarios can be operationally coupled to VBS state and should be modeled separately from a simple VBS-off toggle.

app_current_implementation.notes: The current app writes the documented disabled values for the three Device Guard settings it manages.

records.name: Disable Reserved Storage
records.summary: Official command-backed tweak for disabling Windows Reserved Storage. Microsoft documents reserved storage through DISM command and API surfaces, and the repo already implements that exact model in cleanup.disable-reserved-storage via /Get-ReservedStorageState for detection and /Set-ReservedStorageState /State:Disabled for apply. A local DISM detect run on Windows 11 Pro 10.0.26200.8037 returned 'Reserved storage is disabled.' The internal ReserveManager registry write observed elsewhere is treated as an implementation detail of the documented DISM surface, not the public control surface itself.

decision.why: Microsoft explicitly documents Reserved Storage through DISM command and API surfaces, the current cleanup tweak already implements that exact detect/apply/rollback flow, and a local DISM detect run confirmed the official command surface is live on this Windows 11 build.

app_current_implementation.notes: The current cleanup tweak implements the documented DISM surface directly: detect via /Get-ReservedStorageState, apply via /Set-ReservedStorageState /State:Disabled /NoRestart, and rollback via /State:Enabled when appropriate.

targets.notes: Detection uses DISM.exe /Online /Get-ReservedStorageState. Repo docs and local observation show the command may also drive ReserveManager\DisableDeletes internally, but that registry value is not treated as the public-facing control surface.

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: The registry key allowing long paths is located at HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\FileSystem in the LongPathsEnabled (Type: REG_DWORD) file system registry key. The key will be cached by the system (per process) after the first call to an affected Win32 file or directory function. The registry key will not be reloaded during the lifetime of the process. A value of 1 enables long paths behavior.
validation_proof.notes: Microsoft directly documents LongPathsEnabled as the Windows long-path switch and the app writes that exact documented value. The record is now scoped to the Windows prerequisite itself.

records.name: Global Node.js Memory Limit Override
records.summary: Legacy validated record without machine-checkable validation proof. Officially documented environment-variable based Node.js launch override. The app sets NODE_OPTIONS to use a larger V8 old-space heap size globally, which can help large builds but also increases memory usage for Node.js processes.

records.name: Enable Windows Long Paths for Python Workflows

records.name: Low Disk Space Warning

records.name: Taskbar Chat Icon
records.summary: Microsoft documents the Chat icon through the device policy ConfigureChatIcon under HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Windows Chat. The app implementation was aligned to that official policy surface on 2026-03-13 and now uses ChatIcon=2, the documented Hide state that removes the Chat icon from the taskbar by default while still allowing users to show or hide it in Settings.

decision.why: Local Microsoft policy files document the exact ConfigureChatIcon device-policy surface, enum values, and Hide behavior. The app now writes the same machine-policy path and uses the documented Hide value 2, so the implementation matches the researched control surface. recommended_for_general_users remains false because the practical benefit is mostly cosmetic and Windows defaults may already suit most users.

records.name: Show File Extensions
records.summary: Observed Explorer runtime setting for file-extension visibility. Microsoft Open Specifications documents HideFileExt under Explorer\Advanced, while a reversible Procmon and shell-runtime capture on Windows 11 Pro 10.0.26200.8037 shows Explorer consuming HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\Advanced\HideFileExt as REG_DWORD with 0 = show file extensions and 1 = hide them. The app writes that same runtime surface directly.

decision.why: A reversible runtime capture on Windows 11 Pro 10.0.26200.8037 directly resolved the HideFileExt semantics used by Explorer: 0 shows file extensions and 1 hides them. The app writes the runtime show-extensions state, and the change is a low-risk, user-scope Explorer preference.

app_current_implementation.notes: The app writes HideFileExt = 0 under HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\Advanced. The runtime capture confirmed that 0 is the show-file-extensions state consumed by Explorer on Windows 11 Pro 10.0.26200.8037.

targets.notes: The official path and value name remain useful context, but this record validates the runtime semantics with Procmon and a shell display check because the published wording does not match live Explorer behavior cleanly.

allowed_values.label: Show file extensions
allowed_values.meaning: Explorer displays known file extensions. In the runtime capture, Explorer consumed HideFileExt = 0 after restart and Shell.Application rendered the test file as alpha.txt.

allowed_values.label: Hide file extensions

records.name: Show Hidden Files and Folders
records.summary: Observed Explorer runtime setting for hidden-file visibility. Microsoft Open Specifications documents Hidden under Explorer\Advanced with symbolic SHOW and HIDE semantics, while a reversible Procmon and Explorer UI runtime capture on Windows 11 Pro 10.0.26200.8037 shows Explorer consuming HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\Advanced\Hidden as REG_DWORD with 1 = show hidden items and 2 = hide them. The app writes that same runtime surface directly.

decision.why: A reversible runtime capture on Windows 11 Pro 10.0.26200.8037 directly resolved the Hidden semantics used by Explorer: 1 shows hidden items and 2 hides them. The app writes the runtime show-hidden state. This is a low-risk user-scope Explorer preference, but it is best suited to advanced users.

app_current_implementation.notes: The app writes Hidden = 1 under HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\Advanced. The runtime capture confirmed that 1 is the show-hidden state consumed by Explorer on Windows 11 Pro 10.0.26200.8037.

targets.notes: The official path and value name remain useful context, but this record validates the runtime semantics with Procmon and a direct Explorer UI observation because the published Microsoft source uses symbolic SHOW and HIDE terminology instead of explicit numeric values.

records.name: Taskbar Alignment

decision.why: Procmon on Windows 11 Pro 10.0.26200.8037 confirmed that Explorer consumes HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\Advanced\TaskbarAl as a REG_DWORD runtime setting with 0 = left and 1 = center. The app writes that same surface directly. The official settings reference remains useful feature-level context, but the runtime trace is the decisive proof for the persisted shell value.

app_current_implementation.notes: The app writes TaskbarAl = 0 under HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\Advanced. Procmon confirmed Explorer.EXE consumes the same runtime surface on Windows 11 Pro 10.0.26200.8037.

targets.notes: The Windows 11 settings reference remains useful feature-level context, but this record validates the parent-key REG_DWORD surface because that is what Explorer consumed in the runtime capture.

records.name: IPv6 Stack Disable Override
records.summary: Legacy validated record without machine-checkable validation proof. Sets the official DisabledComponents value to 0xFF, which tells Windows to disable all IPv6 components except the loopback interface. Microsoft warns that disabling IPv6 can break parts of Windows and should be done only when there is a very specific reason.

decision.why: Microsoft directly documents the DisabledComponents registry value under HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\Tcpip6\Parameters, including 0xFF for disabling all IPv6 components except loopback, and explicitly warns that disabling IPv6 is not recommended for general use. The app writes that exact documented value on the documented path.

app_current_implementation.notes: The current app writes DisabledComponents = 255, which matches the documented disable-all-except-loopback setting.

records.name: Set LLTD Policies to Default Behavior
records.summary: The app writes the official LLTD policy values EnableLLTDIO = 0 and EnableRspndr = 0 under HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\LLTD. Microsoft documents those disabled or not configured states as returning to default behavior rather than guaranteeing a hard LLTD-off state. The record is now modeled honestly as setting LLTD policies back to default behavior rather than as a direct LLTD hard-disable.

decision.why: The app writes the documented LLTD policy path and value names, and the UX now reflects Microsoft's actual semantics for value 0: policy-disabled default-behavior handling rather than a guaranteed hard-disable.

app_current_implementation.notes: The app writes the official LLTD policy path and value names, including EnableLLTDIO = 0 and EnableRspndr = 0. The UX now matches the documented semantics: policy-disabled or not configured leaves the default LLTD behavior in place rather than guaranteeing a hard-disable.

allowed_values.label: Default behavior
allowed_values.meaning: The policy is not configured, so Windows uses the default LLTDIO behavior.

allowed_values.label: Policy disabled
allowed_values.meaning: The policy is disabled, which Microsoft says falls back to default LLTDIO behavior.

allowed_values.label: Policy enabled
allowed_values.meaning: The LLTDIO driver is explicitly turned on by policy.

allowed_values.label: Default behavior
allowed_values.meaning: The policy is not configured, so Windows uses the default Responder behavior.

allowed_values.label: Policy disabled
allowed_values.meaning: The policy is disabled, which Microsoft says falls back to default Responder behavior.

allowed_values.label: Policy enabled
allowed_values.meaning: The Responder driver is explicitly turned on by policy.

windows_defaults.label: Policy not configured
windows_defaults.applies_to: Windows Vista and later

states.rationale: Default LLTDIO behavior applies when the policy is not configured.
Default Responder behavior applies when the policy is not configured.

The record is now modeled honestly as setting the mDNS policy back to local-settings behavior rather than as a direct mDNS hard-disable.

decision.why: The app writes the documented mDNS policy path and value name, and the UX now reflects Microsoft's actual semantics for value 0: policy-disabled local-settings behavior rather than a guaranteed hard-disable.

app_current_implementation.notes: The app writes the official mDNS policy surface: EnableMDNS = 0 under HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows NT\DNSClient. The UX now matches the documented semantics: policy-disabled or not configured means the DNS client uses locally configured mDNS behavior.

records.name: NetBIOS over TCP/IP
records.summary: Disables NetBIOS over TCP/IP through the documented per-interface Windows control surface. Official Microsoft documentation exposes NetbiosOptions per adapter with values 0 = DHCP setting, 1 = enabled, 2 = disabled, and also documents the Win32_NetworkAdapterConfiguration.SetTcpipNetbios method for applying that state. The app now uses a command-backed per-interface implementation that sets NetbiosOptions = 2 on all IP-enabled adapters and can restore the previously detected per-adapter state.

decision.why: Official Microsoft documentation is explicit about the real per-interface control surface: NetbiosOptions values 0, 1, and 2 plus the SetTcpipNetbios method. The app now matches that model with a command-backed implementation that enumerates IP-enabled adapters, applies the documented disabled state, and keeps prior per-adapter state for rollback.

app_current_implementation.notes: The app now uses the documented per-interface control model. NetworkTweakProvider exposes the tweak card and delegates apply/verify/rollback to a command-backed tweak that enumerates IP-enabled adapters and applies Win32_NetworkAdapterConfiguration.SetTcpipNetbios(2).

targets.notes: This is adapter-specific. A correct app experience may need to enumerate interfaces instead of pretending the setting is a single global toggle.

allowed_values.label: Use DHCP setting
allowed_values.meaning: Windows follows the NetBIOS preference provided by DHCP for that adapter.

allowed_values.meaning: NetBIOS over TCP/IP is enabled for that adapter.

allowed_values.label: Disabled

evidence.title: Microsoft Learn: detect, enable, and disable SMBv1, SMBv2, and SMBv3 in Windows

evidence.title: Current app implementation
evidence.summary: The provider writes SMB2 = 0 for the current tweak.

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: To enable or disable SMBv2 on an SMB server, open Registry Editor and go to HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\LanmanServer\Parameters. For the name, use SMB2. For the type, use REG_DWORD. For the data, use 0 for Disabled and 1 for Enabled. The default value is 1, or Enabled. In this case, no registry key is created.
validation_proof.notes: Microsoft documents the same LanmanServer registry path, value name, and 0/1 semantics that the app uses for the server-side SMBv2 and SMBv3 disable override.

records.name: IPv4 Preference Override
records.summary: Legacy validated record without machine-checkable validation proof. Sets DisabledComponents to 0x20, which tells Windows to prefer IPv4 over IPv6 without fully disabling the IPv6 stack. This is less disruptive than turning IPv6 off, but it is still an advanced network compatibility override.

decision.why: Microsoft directly documents the DisabledComponents registry value under HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\Tcpip6\Parameters, including 0x20 for preferring IPv4 over IPv6 while keeping IPv6 enabled. The app writes that exact documented value on the documented path.

app_current_implementation.notes: The current app writes DisabledComponents = 32, which matches the Microsoft-documented prefer-IPv4 state.

records.name: SMB Server Leasing
records.summary: Disables SMB server leasing through the documented SMB server configuration surface. Microsoft documents EnableLeasing as a boolean property of MSFT_SmbServerConfiguration and shows Set-SmbServerConfiguration -EnableLeasing $false as the supported way to disable leasing. The app previously wrote DisableLeasing = 1 under the LanmanServer service parameter path; it now uses a command-backed implementation that applies the documented EnableLeasing = false surface and can restore the previously detected boolean state.

decision.why: Microsoft explicitly documents EnableLeasing as the SMB server configuration surface and shows Set-SmbServerConfiguration -EnableLeasing $false as the supported disable method. The app now matches that model with a command-backed implementation that detects the current boolean state, applies the documented disable action, verifies the result, and restores the previously detected value on rollback.

app_current_implementation.notes: NetworkTweakProvider now exposes the tweak card and delegates apply, verify, and rollback to a command-backed SMB server configuration implementation that detects EnableLeasing and applies Set-SmbServerConfiguration -EnableLeasing $false.

writes.notes: Applied through Set-SmbServerConfiguration -EnableLeasing $false and restored to the previously detected boolean state during rollback.

targets.notes: This is the documented SMB server management surface. The app now applies the false state via Set-SmbServerConfiguration -EnableLeasing $false.

records.summary: Enables SMB Multichannel through the documented SMB client and SMB server configuration surfaces. records.name: SMB over QUIC
records.summary: Enables SMB over QUIC by writing EnableSMBQUIC = 1 under the documented LanmanWorkstation and LanmanServer policy keys. Local Microsoft LanmanWorkstation.admx and LanmanServer.admx define the same value name with enabledValue 1 and disabledValue 0. The app previously wrote the same value name under service parameter paths; it now targets the official policy surface directly.

decision.why: Local official ADMX and ADML files document the exact EnableSMBQUIC policy surfaces and values for both LanmanWorkstation and LanmanServer, and the app now writes those documented policy keys directly.

app_current_implementation.notes: The app now writes EnableSMBQUIC = 1 under the documented LanmanWorkstation and LanmanServer policy keys.

records.name: SMB Server Encryption Requirement
records.summary: Legacy validated record now backed by a machine-checkable guest reversible probe. Controls the global SMB server EncryptData value under LanmanServer. Requiring encryption is a strong hardening step for hosted shares, but older SMB clients and some environments can lose compatibility or pay a performance cost.

decision.why: A guest-side reversible probe on Win25H2Clean confirmed the EncryptData mapping through the documented server configuration surface: False -> True -> False -> restored False. The current app write matches the documented global SMB encryption requirement. The apply decision remains conservative because older SMB clients and some environments can lose compatibility or pay a performance cost.

app_current_implementation.notes: The current app writes EncryptData = 1, which matches the documented global SMB server encryption requirement.

targets.notes: This record documents the global LanmanServer value only, not every SMB share-level encryption control.

allowed_values.label: Windows managed baseline
allowed_values.meaning: Windows keeps its SMB server encryption baseline without an explicit local override in this registry value.

allowed_values.label: Global encryption not required
allowed_values.meaning: The server does not force global SMB encryption through this registry override.

allowed_values.label: Global encryption required
allowed_values.meaning: The server requires SMB encryption for shared data through this registry override.

records.name: SMB Server Reject Unencrypted Access
records.summary: Controls the global SMB server RejectUnencryptedAccess value under LanmanServer. This is a strict hardening step that refuses clients not using SMB encryption, but it can break legacy clients and mixed environments.

decision.why: Microsoft documents the exact registry control used by the app for rejecting unencrypted SMB access, even though broader SMB encryption behavior can also be managed through other SMB surfaces.

records.name: Require SMB Dialect 3.1.1
records.summary: Restricts SMB client and server dialect negotiation to exactly SMB 3.1.1 by writing MinSmb2Dialect = 785 and MaxSmb2Dialect = 785 under the documented LanmanWorkstation and LanmanServer policy keys. Local Microsoft LanmanWorkstation.admx and LanmanServer.admx define the official enum values for both MinSmb2Dialect and MaxSmb2Dialect, where 785 corresponds to SMB 3.1.1. The app previously wrote service-path values and used an undocumented MaxSmb2Dialect value of 65536; it now targets the official policy surface directly.

decision.why: Local official ADMX and ADML files document the exact MinSmb2Dialect and MaxSmb2Dialect policy surfaces and official enum values for both LanmanWorkstation and LanmanServer, and the app now writes the documented SMB 3.1.1 value directly to those policy keys.

app_current_implementation.notes: The app now writes MinSmb2Dialect = 785 and MaxSmb2Dialect = 785 under the documented LanmanWorkstation and LanmanServer policy keys, which enforces exactly SMB 3.1.1 on both client and server policy surfaces.

records.name: SMB Client Signing Requirement
records.summary: Requires SMB signing for outbound client connections by setting both RequireSecuritySignature and EnableSecuritySignature under LanmanWorkstation. Microsoft directly documents the same registry key, value names, and 0/1 semantics that the app uses for this SMB client signing posture.

decision.why: Microsoft directly documents HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Services\LanManWorkstation\Parameters -> RequireSecuritySignature and EnableSecuritySignature, both with Data 0 (disable) and 1 (enable). The same page also explains that SMB2+ signing is ultimately controlled by RequireSecuritySignature, while EnableSecuritySignature is ignored for SMB2+ but still a documented runtime setting on the same surface.

app_current_implementation.notes: The current app writes RequireSecuritySignature = 1 and EnableSecuritySignature = 1, matching the stricter SMB client signing posture described by Microsoft.

Registry key: HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\System\CurrentControlSet\Services\LanManWorkstation\Parameters; Registry value: EnableSecuritySignature; Data Type: REG_DWORD; Data: 0 (disable), 1 (enable).
validation_proof.notes: Microsoft documents both client-side SMB signing registry values on the same LanManWorkstation runtime path the app writes. The article also notes that EnableSecuritySignature is ignored for SMB2+ and that SMB2.02 and later signing is controlled by being required or not.

records.name: SMB Server Signing Requirement
records.summary: Requires SMB signing for inbound server connections by setting both RequireSecuritySignature and EnableSecuritySignature under LanmanServer. Microsoft directly documents the same registry key, value names, and 0/1 semantics that the app uses for this SMB server signing posture.

decision.why: Microsoft directly documents HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Services\LanManServer\Parameters -> RequireSecuritySignature and explains that SMB server signing is controlled through the same LanManServer runtime path the app writes. The same page also explains that for SMB2 and later, EnableSecuritySignature is ignored and signing is controlled by being required or not, so the app's extra EnableSecuritySignature write stays on the documented surface even if it is redundant for modern SMB.

app_current_implementation.notes: The current app writes RequireSecuritySignature = 1 and EnableSecuritySignature = 1, matching the documented stricter SMB server signing posture.

records.name: Disable Window Animations
records.summary: Microsoft documents window animation behavior through SystemParametersInfo(SPI_GETANIMATION / SPI_SETANIMATION) and ANIMATIONINFO.iMinAnimate. A local runtime diff on 2026-03-14 confirmed that calling SPI_SETANIMATION with animations enabled persisted HKCU\Control Panel\Desktop\WindowMetrics\MinAnimate = "1", while calling SPI_SETANIMATION with animations disabled persisted MinAnimate = "0". The current app write of MinAnimate = "0" therefore matches the observed persisted backend for the documented Win32 feature surface on this build.

decision.why: The feature semantics are officially documented through the Win32 animation API, and a local runtime diff now proves that MinAnimate is the persisted backend for the enabled and disabled states on this build. The remaining limitation is documentation quality: MinAnimate was validated through API-backed runtime observation rather than a Microsoft-published registry contract.

app_current_implementation.notes: The app writes MinAnimate = "0" under HKCU\Control Panel\Desktop\WindowMetrics. A local runtime diff confirmed that this matches the persisted disabled state produced by the official Win32 animation API surface on this build.

targets.notes: Validated as an API-backed runtime registry mapping. No Microsoft page captured in this pass publishes MinAnimate directly as the supported registry contract.

allowed_values.label: Window animations disabled
allowed_values.meaning: A local SPI_SETANIMATION runtime diff confirmed that disabling the official Win32 animation feature persists MinAnimate = "0" on this build.

allowed_values.label: Window animations enabled
allowed_values.meaning: A local SPI_SETANIMATION runtime diff confirmed that enabling the official Win32 animation feature persists MinAnimate = "1" on this build.

windows_defaults.applies_to: Windows user profiles where this pass did not capture the absent-value default for MinAnimate

avoid_for: Users who prefer smoother visual transitions

recommended_profiles.bad_when: You want the default animated minimize and restore experience.
tradeoffs: Windows transitions look more abrupt.
The desktop can feel faster or more responsive.

evidence.title: Local SPI_SETANIMATION runtime diff - MinAnimate persistence
evidence.summary: A local runtime diff called SPI_SETANIMATION with animations disabled and enabled, then read both SPI_GETANIMATION and HKCU\Control Panel\Desktop\WindowMetrics\MinAnimate after each change. Disabled stayed at API false with MinAnimate = "0". Enabled switched to API true with MinAnimate = "1". The original local state was restored at the end.

evidence.title: Current app implementation
evidence.summary: The provider writes MinAnimate = "0" under HKCU\Control Panel\Desktop\WindowMetrics.

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: after_disable: API false, registry MinAnimate = "0". after_enable: API true, registry MinAnimate = "1". restored: API false, registry MinAnimate = "0".
validation_proof.notes: The local runtime diff used the official SystemParametersInfo animation API to toggle the feature and then read the persisted registry backend. The machine ended in its original state.

records.name: Remove Menu Show Delay
records.summary: Microsoft documents menu show delay through SystemParametersInfo with SPI_GETMENUSHOWDELAY and SPI_SETMENUSHOWDELAY. A local runtime diff on 2026-03-14 confirmed that calling SPI_SETMENUSHOWDELAY with 400 persisted HKCU\Control Panel\Desktop\MenuShowDelay = "400", while calling SPI_SETMENUSHOWDELAY with 0 persisted MenuShowDelay = "0". The current app write of MenuShowDelay = "0" therefore matches the observed persisted backend for the documented Win32 feature surface on this build.

decision.why: The menu-delay feature semantics are officially documented through the Win32 API, and a local runtime diff now proves that MenuShowDelay is the persisted backend for the tested delayed and zero-delay states on this build. The remaining limitation is documentation quality: MenuShowDelay was validated through API-backed runtime observation rather than a Microsoft-published registry contract.

app_current_implementation.notes: The app writes MenuShowDelay = "0" under HKCU\Control Panel\Desktop. A local runtime diff confirmed that this matches the persisted zero-delay state produced by the official Win32 menu-delay API on this build.

records.name: Disable Sticky Keys Prompt
records.summary: Win32 officially documents Sticky Keys through SystemParametersInfo and the STICKYKEYS flag structure. A local runtime diff on 2026-03-14 confirmed that calling SPI_SETSTICKYKEYS with flags 510 persisted HKCU\Control Panel\Accessibility\StickyKeys\Flags = "510", while calling SPI_SETSTICKYKEYS with flags 506 persisted Flags = "506". The current app write of Flags = "506" therefore matches the observed persisted backend for the documented Sticky Keys feature surface on this build, and the difference between 510 and 506 is the SKF_HOTKEYACTIVE bit.

decision.why: The Sticky Keys feature semantics are officially documented through the Win32 accessibility API, and a local runtime diff now proves that the app's Flags = "506" profile is the persisted backend for the documented Sticky Keys feature state on this build. The remaining limitation is documentation quality: the registry path was validated through API-backed runtime observation rather than a Microsoft-published registry contract.

app_current_implementation.notes: The app writes Flags = "506" under HKCU\Control Panel\Accessibility\StickyKeys. A local runtime diff confirmed that this matches the persisted API-backed profile that leaves SKF_HOTKEYACTIVE cleared on this build.

targets.notes: Validated as an API-backed runtime registry mapping. No Microsoft page captured in this pass publishes the StickyKeys Flags registry path directly as the supported contract.

allowed_values.label: Sticky Keys prompt-disabled profile
allowed_values.meaning: A local SPI_SETSTICKYKEYS runtime diff confirmed that Flags = "506" is the persisted backend when the documented Sticky Keys flag set does not include SKF_HOTKEYACTIVE. This suppresses the Shift hotkey path that would otherwise trigger the prompt.

allowed_values.label: Sticky Keys hotkey-active profile
allowed_values.meaning: A local SPI_SETSTICKYKEYS runtime diff confirmed that Flags = "510" is the persisted backend when the documented Sticky Keys flag set includes SKF_HOTKEYACTIVE.

windows_defaults.applies_to: Windows user profiles where StickyKeys Flags is present

intended_for: Users who trigger Sticky Keys prompts accidentally
Gamers and fast typists who do not want repeated-Shift interruptions
avoid_for: Users who rely on the Sticky Keys shortcut to discover or enable the accessibility feature

states.rationale: Set the observed persisted registry backend to the profile with SKF_HOTKEYACTIVE cleared.

recommended_profiles.bad_when: You want the Sticky Keys hotkey path to stay available.
tradeoffs: Repeated Shift presses no longer follow the hotkey-active path that triggers the prompt.
The accessibility shortcut becomes less discoverable.

records.name: Fast Startup (Hiberboot)
records.summary: Disables the local Fast Startup setting by writing HiberbootEnabled under HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Session Manager\Power. This is the documented local on/off surface for Fast Startup. A separate policy value, HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\System\HiberbootEnabled, only forces Fast Startup on and does not provide a symmetric disable path. The app previously wrote HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Power\HiberbootEnabled, which is not the authoritative local Fast Startup toggle; both app surfaces now align to the Session Manager path.

decision.why: The local Fast Startup toggle is explicitly documented by Microsoft at HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Session Manager\Power\HiberbootEnabled, with 0 meaning disabled and 1 meaning enabled. The separate WinInit policy path only forces Fast Startup on and falls back to the local setting when disabled or not configured. The app provider and engine helper now both write the documented local surface, so the implementation matches the researched behavior.

app_current_implementation.notes: The app provider and engine helper both write HiberbootEnabled=0 under HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Session Manager\Power. This matches the documented local Fast Startup setting. The previously used Control\Power path was corrected on 2026-03-13.

targets.notes: Do not confuse the local Fast Startup toggle with the separate WinInit policy HiberbootEnabled under HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\System. The policy can force Fast Startup on, but disabled or not configured falls back to the local setting.

allowed_values.label: Windows managed default
allowed_values.meaning: Windows chooses the Fast Startup baseline based on OS configuration and hibernation capability.

records.name: Optimize CPU Performance Boost
records.summary: Command-backed CPU boost tweak that uses the documented PERFBOOSTMODE power setting instead of the old raw registry bundle. Microsoft publishes PERFBOOSTMODE as a supported powercfg surface, and the guest reversible probe on Win25H2Clean confirmed the active scheme can be read, moved to 1 and 2, and restored back to the original state.

decision.why: Microsoft documents PERFBOOSTMODE as a supported powercfg surface with Aggressive at index 2, the app now uses that documented surface directly, and a guest reversible probe on Win25H2Clean confirmed the active scheme can move through 1 and 2 and restore back to the original state.

app_current_implementation.notes: The current app implementation is command-backed. It detects the current active power plan's PERFBOOSTMODE values, applies PERFBOOSTMODE=2 through powercfg, verifies the live state, and restores the previously detected AC/DC values on rollback.

writes.path: powercfg.exe /setacvalueindex SCHEME_CURRENT SUB_PROCESSOR PERFBOOSTMODE 2 /setdcvalueindex SCHEME_CURRENT SUB_PROCESSOR PERFBOOSTMODE 2 /setactive SCHEME_CURRENT
writes.notes: Detected state is serialized as the previous AC/DC indices and restored on rollback.

targets.path: powercfg.exe /setacvalueindex SCHEME_CURRENT SUB_PROCESSOR PERFBOOSTMODE 2 /setdcvalueindex SCHEME_CURRENT SUB_PROCESSOR PERFBOOSTMODE 2 /setactive SCHEME_CURRENT

allowed_values.label: Disabled
allowed_values.meaning: PowerCfg alias PERFBOOSTMODE index 0 disables boosting.

allowed_values.label: Enabled
allowed_values.meaning: PowerCfg alias PERFBOOSTMODE index 1 enables boosting.

allowed_values.label: Aggressive
allowed_values.meaning: PowerCfg alias PERFBOOSTMODE index 2 enables aggressive boosting.

allowed_values.label: Same as 1
allowed_values.meaning: PowerCfg alias PERFBOOSTMODE index 3 behaves the same as index 1.

allowed_values.label: Same as 2
allowed_values.meaning: PowerCfg alias PERFBOOSTMODE index 4 behaves the same as index 2.

windows_defaults.label: Windows-managed boost baseline
windows_defaults.applies_to: Windows systems using normal processor boost management

states.rationale: The active scheme can vary by machine, so the current PERFBOOSTMODE state should be observed rather than assumed.

recommended_profiles.label: Windows default
intended_for: Systems using supported Windows processor boost management
avoid_for: Systems where the operator explicitly wants a more aggressive boost profile

states.rationale: Keep boost behavior under the normal Windows-managed baseline.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want supported Windows processor boost behavior.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You are trying to force aggressive boost tuning without a specific performance reason.
tradeoffs: Windows keeps its supported boost-management path.
No explicit boost override is pushed through powercfg.

records.name: Disable Application Compatibility Engine and SwitchBack
records.summary: Two AppCompat.admx policy values written by the app under HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\AppCompat: SbEnable (SwitchBack) and DisableEngine (Application Compatibility Engine). Both come from the same policy family and the same registry root. Procmon + LGPO capture on 2026-03-13 confirmed svchost.exe -k GPSvcGroup (gpsvc) writes SbEnable=REG_DWORD 0x0 and DisableEngine=REG_DWORD 0x1 on Windows 11 Pro 10.0.26200.8037. Microsoft Learn CSP page (Policy CSP - ADMX_AppCompat) confirms both policies exist on Windows 11 21H2+. SbEnable is now also published as a standalone record in privacy.disable-switchback.policy; this record keeps it for bundle-audit context because the current app still writes both values together.

decision.why: Both values are now proven in three layers: local ADMX semantics, the Microsoft Learn ADMX_AppCompat CSP page, and Procmon capture showing gpsvc writes DisableEngine=1 and SbEnable=0 under HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\AppCompat on Windows 11 Pro 10.0.26200.8037. Proof is no longer the blocker; risk is. Microsoft ADML explicitly warns that disabling the Application Compatibility Engine can cause BSOD when old antivirus or other security software is installed, which keeps apply_allowed=false for this combined bundle.

app_current_implementation.notes: The app writes both values as part of the broader application-compatibility policy path. Paths and values match local AppCompat.admx. SbEnable is now documented separately in privacy.disable-switchback.policy for standalone publication, but it remains here because the current app still writes it together with DisableEngine. The app also writes four AppDeviceInventory values and DisablePCA in the same code path; those are tracked in separate records.

targets.notes: Windows default is missing (key absent). SbEnable is the cleanest proven value in this record. Procmon confirmed 2026-03-13 on Win11 Pro 10.0.26200.8037: svchost.exe (gpsvc) writes SbEnable=REG_DWORD 0x0 when AppCompatTurnOffSwitchBack policy is enabled via LGPO + gpupdate. Numeric direction is fully proven. Standalone publication guidance now lives in privacy.disable-switchback.policy; this target remains here to document the current combined app bundle.

allowed_values.label: Not configured
allowed_values.meaning: Windows keeps the normal SwitchBack behavior. SwitchBack allows applications that detect Windows Vista or later to also receive compatibility fixes normally reserved for older OS versions.

allowed_values.label: SwitchBack enabled (policy explicit)
allowed_values.meaning: disabledValue in AppCompat.admx. SwitchBack runs normally. Equivalent to missing.

allowed_values.label: Turn off SwitchBack
allowed_values.meaning: enabledValue in AppCompat.admx. SwitchBack is disabled. This is the value the app writes. Numeric direction is explicitly confirmed via the ADMX enabledValue block. Procmon confirmed: svchost.exe (gpsvc) writes SbEnable=REG_DWORD 0x0 during gpupdate /force on 2026-03-13 (Windows 11 Pro 10.0.26200.8037).

targets.notes: Windows default is missing (key absent). Procmon confirmed 2026-03-13 on Win11 Pro 10.0.26200.8037: svchost.exe (gpsvc) writes DisableEngine=REG_DWORD 0x1 when AppCompatTurnOffEngine policy is enabled via LGPO + gpupdate. Numeric direction proven; explicit ADMX enabledValue block absence is no longer a blocker.

allowed_values.label: Not configured
allowed_values.meaning: Windows keeps the normal Application Compatibility Engine behavior. The engine checks a compatibility database each time an application starts and may apply run-time fixes, compatibility shims, or display Application Help messages for known incompatible applications.

allowed_values.label: Turn off Application Compatibility Engine

Value=1 is consistent with AppCompat.admx policy behavior, and Procmon confirmed svchost.exe (gpsvc) writes DisableEngine=REG_DWORD 0x1 during gpupdate /force on 2026-03-13 (Windows 11 Pro 10.0.26200.8037). Microsoft ADML explicitly states this may cause BSOD if old antivirus software is installed.

evidence.title: Procmon + LGPO capture - AppCompatTurnOffEngine and AppCompatTurnOffSwitchBack
evidence.location: Local capture - 2026-03-13, Windows 11 Pro 10.0.26200.8037 (OsName: Microsoft Windows 11 Pro)
evidence.summary: LGPO.exe applied six AppCompat policy values. Procmon captured svchost.exe -k GPSvcGroup (gpsvc) writing to HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\AppCompat during gpupdate /force. RegSetValue confirmed DisableEngine=REG_DWORD 0x1 and SbEnable=REG_DWORD 0x0. reg query after gpupdate showed both values present at expected paths. Rollback removed Registry.pol, deleted both values with reg delete, and gpupdate /force returned the key to AITEnable=0x0 only. Capture file: C:\Users\<USER>\AppData\Local\Temp\appcompat_policy_capture_lgpo_clean.pml

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: AppCompat.admx: `<policy name="AppCompatTurnOffSwitchBack" ... key="Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\AppCompat" valueName="SbEnable">` with `<enabledValue><decimal value="0" /></enabledValue>` and `<disabledValue><decimal value="1" /></disabledValue>`; AppCompat.admx: `<policy name="AppCompatTurnOffEngine" ... key="Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\AppCompat" valueName="DisableEngine">`; AppCompat.adml: `If you enable this policy setting, Switchback will be turned off.` and `If you enable this policy setting, the Application Compatibility Engine will be turned off.`
validation_proof.notes: Local official AppCompat.admx and AppCompat.adml files document the exact SbEnable and DisableEngine policy surfaces. Procmon then confirmed gpsvc writes SbEnable=REG_DWORD 0x0 and DisableEngine=REG_DWORD 0x1 on Windows 11 Pro 10.0.26200.8037.

records.name: Disable App Device Inventory Policies
records.summary: Four AppDeviceInventory policy values written by the app under HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\AppCompat. All four come from the same policy family (AppDeviceInventory.admx), share the same registry root, and have explicit enabledValue=1 / disabledValue=0 blocks in the local ADMX. Microsoft Learn CSP page (Policy CSP - AppDeviceInventory) confirms these policies exist. Procmon + LGPO capture on 2026-03-13 confirmed svchost.exe -k GPSvcGroup (gpsvc) writes all four values under HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\AppCompat on Windows 11 Pro 10.0.26200.8037. Derived from the former mixed application-compatibility policy record on 2026-03-13.

decision.why: All four values are now confirmed in three layers: local AppDeviceInventory.admx (explicit enabledValue=1), the Microsoft Learn CSP page, and Procmon capture showing gpsvc writes all four values under HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\AppCompat on Windows 11 Pro 10.0.26200.8037. Risk is low because these policies reduce telemetry collection rather than core application compatibility behavior. Pre-24H2 systems may ignore the keys, but that is a scope caveat rather than a safety blocker.

app_current_implementation.notes: The app writes all four values as part of the broader application-compatibility policy path. Path and values match both local AppDeviceInventory.admx and the CSP page. The app still writes DisableEngine, SbEnable, and DisablePCA in the same code path; those are tracked in separate records.

evidence.title: Procmon + LGPO capture - four AppDeviceInventory policies
evidence.location: Local capture - 2026-03-13, Windows 11 Pro 10.0.26200.8037 (OsName: Microsoft Windows 11 Pro, OsVersion: 10.0.26200)
evidence.summary: LGPO.exe applied six AppCompat policy values including all four AppDeviceInventory values. Procmon captured svchost.exe -k GPSvcGroup (gpsvc) writing to HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\AppCompat during gpupdate /force. RegSetValue confirmed DisableAPISamping=REG_DWORD 0x1, DisableApplicationFootprint=REG_DWORD 0x1, DisableInstallTracing=REG_DWORD 0x1, and DisableWin32AppBackup=REG_DWORD 0x1. reg query after gpupdate showed all four values present at expected paths. Rollback removed Registry.pol, deleted the values with reg delete, and gpupdate /force returned the key to AITEnable=0x0 only. Capture file: C:\Users\<USER>\AppData\Local\Temp\appcompat_policy_capture_lgpo_clean.pml

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: AppDeviceInventory.admx: `<policy name="TurnOffInstallTracing" ... key="Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\AppCompat" valueName="DisableInstallTracing">`, `<policy name="TurnOffAPISamping" ... valueName="DisableAPISamping">`, `<policy name="TurnOffApplicationFootprint" ... valueName="DisableApplicationFootprint">`, and `<policy name="TurnOffWin32AppBackup" ... valueName="DisableWin32AppBackup">`, each with `<enabledValue><decimal value="1" /></enabledValue>` and `<disabledValue><decimal value="0" /></disabledValue>` and `supportedOn ref="windows:SUPPORTED_Windows_11_0_24H2"`.
validation_proof.notes: Local official AppDeviceInventory.admx documents all four values under HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\AppCompat with explicit enabledValue=1 and disabledValue=0, and Procmon confirmed gpsvc writes all four values on Windows 11 Pro 10.0.26200.8037.

records.name: PowerShell and .NET CLI Telemetry Opt-Out
records.summary: Legacy validated record without machine-checkable validation proof. Officially documented user environment-variable bundle for opting PowerShell and the .NET CLI out of telemetry. Good for privacy-focused shell workflows, but it can reduce telemetry available for product improvement and support diagnostics.

decision.why: Microsoft directly documents POWERSHELL_TELEMETRY_OPTOUT and DOTNET_CLI_TELEMETRY_OPTOUT as supported opt-out environment variables, and documents that persistent user environment variables are stored under HKEY_USERS\<user>\Environment, which corresponds to HKCU\Environment for the current user. The app writes the documented opt-out value 1 for both variables on that exact user environment surface.

app_current_implementation.notes: The current app writes both documented opt-out variables as REG_SZ value 1 under HKCU\Environment.

targets.notes: HKCU\Environment is the current-user view of the persistent user environment-variable store.

records.name: Windows Copilot
records.summary: User policy for legacy Windows Copilot. Microsoft documents the same user policy path and value name that the app writes, but the policy is deprecated and does not apply to the newer Copilot experience rolling out on some newer Windows builds.

The app writes that exact user policy surface, and the policy is deprecated for the newer Copilot experience. A Win25H2Clean surface discovery found no Copilot app or Start menu entry to exercise, so there is no additional guest-side runtime surface to validate on this build.

records.name: Disable PCA Compatibility Diagnostics Detection
records.summary: Controls whether Windows Program Compatibility Assistant (PCA) detects compatibility issues for applications and drivers. Value name: DisablePcaUI under HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\AppCompat. Policy family: pca.admx (DisablePcaUIPolicy). Supported on Windows 8+. enabledValue=1 (detection active, explicit policy), disabledValue=0 (detection disabled). To disable diagnostics, the correct write value is 0 - the value name 'DisablePcaUI' is misleading. Note: if 'Turn off Program Compatibility Assistant' (DisablePCA=1) is active, this policy has no effect. This record was created on 2026-03-13 after DisablePcaUI was found to be the wrong key in the previous disable-program-compatibility-assistant record (which correctly targets DisablePCA).

decision.why: The proof chain is complete: pca.admx provides explicit enabledValue and disabledValue mappings, pca.adml provides exact behavior text, and the Microsoft Learn CSP page confirms the policy surface. Numeric direction is explicit and does not rely on inference. Risk is low because this disables diagnostics detection rather than disabling PCA entirely. recommended_for_general_users remains false because the practical benefit is small and the setting needs context to use responsibly.

app_current_implementation.notes: The app does not currently write DisablePcaUI. Previously, PrivacyTweakProvider.cs wrote DisablePcaUI=1 intending to disable PCA - this was incorrect on two levels: wrong key (should be DisablePCA) and wrong value (1 means detection active, not disabled). That was corrected on 2026-03-13 to write DisablePCA=1. This record tracks DisablePcaUI as a standalone diagnostics policy with correct semantics. No app mapping currently exists for this policy.

targets.notes: WARNING: value name 'DisablePcaUI' is semantically inverted relative to its behavior. To disable detection, write 0 (disabledValue), not 1. Writing 1 (enabledValue) keeps detection active. This is consistent with pca.admx but counterintuitive from the value name alone. Do not confuse with DisablePCA (separate key, same path) which actually disables PCA entirely.

allowed_values.label: Not configured
allowed_values.meaning: PCA detects compatibility issues for applications and drivers. Default Windows behavior.

allowed_values.label: Disable PCA diagnostics detection
allowed_values.meaning: disabledValue in pca.admx. PCA does not detect compatibility issues. Detection and related diagnostics are suppressed. Note: has no effect if DisablePCA=1 is active.

allowed_values.label: PCA diagnostics detection active (policy explicit)
allowed_values.meaning: enabledValue in pca.admx. PCA detects compatibility issues. Equivalent to missing/not configured.

windows_defaults.label: Windows managed default
windows_defaults.applies_to: Windows 8+

states.rationale: Key absent. PCA detects compatibility issues normally.

states.rationale: disabledValue confirmed in pca.admx. PCA stops detecting compatibility issues. Write 0 to disable detection - writing 1 would keep detection active.

recommended_profiles.bad_when: Running older or unknown software where compatibility warnings may be meaningful.
tradeoffs: PCA will not warn about compatibility issues even if they occur.
No known BSOD risk - this is isolated to DisablePcaUI=0, not DisablePCA.
Reversible: remove key or set to 1 and run gpupdate /force.
Has no effect if DisablePCA=1 is active - PCA is already fully disabled in that case.
recommended_profiles.notes: apply_allowed=true. The proof chain is complete: pca.admx explicit enabledValue=1 and disabledValue=0, pca.adml exact behavior text, and the Microsoft Learn ADMX_pca CSP page. Risk is low because this only disables diagnostics detection and does not disable PCA entirely. Procmon capture is optional here because the explicit ADMX numeric mapping is already sufficient for this class of record.

records.name: Disable SwitchBack Compatibility Policy
records.summary: Controls the SwitchBack policy under HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\AppCompat. SwitchBack allows applications that detect Windows Vista or later to also receive compatibility fixes normally reserved for older OS versions. SbEnable=0 disables this behavior. Extracted from privacy.disable-appcompat-engine.policy on 2026-03-13 because SbEnable is individually proven and does not carry the Microsoft-documented BSOD risk tied to DisableEngine in the parent bundle. AppCompat.admx has explicit enabledValue=0 and disabledValue=1 for SbEnable, the Microsoft Learn CSP page confirms the policy exists, and Procmon confirmed svchost.exe (gpsvc) writes SbEnable=REG_DWORD 0x0 during gpupdate /force on Windows 11 Pro 10.0.26200.8037.

decision.why: Proof chain is complete and no comparable BSOD or engine-wide compatibility risk is documented for SbEnable. AppCompat.admx provides explicit enabledValue=0, the Microsoft Learn CSP page confirms policy existence and scope, and Procmon confirmed the runtime write. recommended_for_general_users remains false because the practical benefit is small and most users do not need to manage this policy explicitly.

app_current_implementation.notes: The app writes SbEnable=0 as part of the broader application-compatibility policy bundle. This record documents SbEnable in isolation after extraction from privacy.disable-appcompat-engine.policy. Path and value match local AppCompat.admx and Procmon capture. The app still writes DisableEngine=1 in the same code path; that value is tracked separately in privacy.disable-appcompat-engine.policy.

targets.notes: Windows default is missing (key absent). Missing behaves identically to value=1. Explicit enabledValue=0 in AppCompat.admx proves numeric direction without context inference. No BSOD risk is documented for SbEnable alone; that warning belongs to DisableEngine in the parent bundle.

allowed_values.label: Not configured
allowed_values.meaning: Windows keeps the normal SwitchBack behavior. Applications that detect Windows Vista or later can receive compatibility fixes designed for older OS versions.

allowed_values.label: SwitchBack enabled (policy explicit)
allowed_values.meaning: disabledValue in AppCompat.admx. SwitchBack runs normally. Equivalent to missing.

allowed_values.label: Turn off SwitchBack
allowed_values.meaning: enabledValue in AppCompat.admx. SwitchBack is disabled. Applications that detect Windows Vista or later no longer receive cross-version compatibility fixes. Procmon confirmed svchost.exe (gpsvc) writes SbEnable=REG_DWORD 0x0 during gpupdate /force on Windows 11 Pro 10.0.26200.8037 (2026-03-13).

evidence.title: Procmon + LGPO capture - AppCompatTurnOffSwitchBack
evidence.summary: LGPO.exe applied SbEnable=0 via local policy. Procmon captured svchost.exe -k GPSvcGroup (gpsvc) writing HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\AppCompat\SbEnable=REG_DWORD 0x0 during gpupdate /force. reg query confirmed the value at the expected path. records.name: Start Menu Recommended Section (Current User)
records.summary: User policy for the Recommended section in the Windows 11 Start menu. The local Microsoft StartMenu.admx file explicitly documents the same Explorer policy key, HideRecommendedSection value name, and Both-scope surface that the app uses for the current user.

decision.why: The local Microsoft StartMenu.admx file explicitly defines HideRecommendedSection under Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Explorer with class="Both", which means the same policy surface is valid for user scope as well as device scope. The official Start Policy CSP provides the 0/1 semantics, and the app writes that documented current-user hide value.

app_current_implementation.notes: The current app writes HideRecommendedSection = 1 under HKCU, which matches the documented hide behavior.

allowed_values.meaning: The section is hidden for the current user.

records.name: Display of Username During Sign-In
records.summary: Research record for hiding username information during sign-in. The app writes DontDisplayUserName, and the local Windows security metadata in sceregvl.inf confirms that exact registry surface for the documented security option.

decision.why: Microsoft documents the sign-in privacy behavior and the guest-side VM probe confirmed that sceregvl.inf exposes DontDisplayUserName as the registry surface for this option. The app already writes that exact value, so the record is now validated.

app_current_implementation.notes: The current app writes DontDisplayUserName = 1, and the guest-side probe on Win25H2Clean confirmed that sceregvl.inf maps the documented security option to DontDisplayUserName. The earlier DontDisplayLockedUserId interpretation was a different nearby security option entry, not the surface used by this tweak.

allowed_values.label: Keep username visible during sign-in
allowed_values.meaning: The security option is not enabled, so the username can remain visible during the sign-in flow.

allowed_values.label: Hide username during sign-in
allowed_values.meaning: The security option is enabled and the username is not displayed after credentials are entered.

recommended_profiles.bad_when: You want less identity information shown on screen while signing in.
tradeoffs: Users get a clearer confirmation of which account is signing in.
The sign-in screen exposes more identity information.

avoid_for: Users who prefer more visible sign-in confirmation

tradeoffs: Identity exposure during sign-in is reduced.

records.name: Advanced Diagnostic Log Collection
records.summary: Machine policy for limiting advanced diagnostic log collection. The local Microsoft DataCollection.admx file explicitly documents the same DataCollection policy key and LimitDiagnosticLogCollection value name that the app uses, and the ADML help text explains the effect.

decision.why: The local Microsoft DataCollection.admx file explicitly defines LimitDiagnosticLogCollection under Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\DataCollection with valueName LimitDiagnosticLogCollection, and the paired ADML help text states that enabling the policy stops additional diagnostic logs from being collected while disabled or not configured may allow them. The app writes that documented limiting value.

app_current_implementation.notes: The current app writes LimitDiagnosticLogCollection = 1, which matches the documented limit behavior.

records.name: Set Diagnostic Data to Minimum Supported Level
records.summary: The app writes AllowTelemetry = 0 and now labels it as setting diagnostic data to the minimum supported level. Microsoft documents that value 0 is only supported on Enterprise, Education, and Server editions, so the renamed UX is edition-aware instead of implying a general-purpose off switch.

decision.why: The official mapping is clear and the provider label now matches the documented lowest supported diagnostic-data level. The tweak remains not recommended for general users because value 0 has edition-limited semantics.

app_current_implementation.notes: The current app writes AllowTelemetry = 0 and the provider label now reflects the documented lowest supported diagnostic-data level. The implementation matches the research and stays edition-aware through the documented policy semantics.

allowed_values.label: Not configured
allowed_values.meaning: Windows sends required diagnostic data and the user can choose whether to send optional diagnostic data.

allowed_values.label: Diagnostic data off (limited edition support)
allowed_values.meaning: Microsoft documents this as turning diagnostic data off, but only on Enterprise, Education, and Server editions.

allowed_values.label: Required diagnostic data
allowed_values.meaning: Windows sends the minimum diagnostic data needed to keep Windows secure, updated, and functioning as expected.

allowed_values.label: Optional diagnostic data
allowed_values.meaning: Windows sends required diagnostic data plus additional diagnostic data such as broader logs and crash data.

windows_defaults.applies_to: Windows 10 and Windows 11 systems exposing the diagnostic-data policy

avoid_for: Enterprise or education devices that explicitly want the minimum possible level

recommended_profiles.bad_when: You need an explicit organization-managed diagnostic-data policy.
tradeoffs: Windows sends required diagnostic data.
The user can still choose optional diagnostic data.

intended_for: Organizations that want the lowest supported documented level
avoid_for: General consumer editions without SKU-aware gating

states.rationale: Use the lowest documented value only where Microsoft documents it as supported.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You have edition-aware logic and know the device supports value 0 as documented.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You treat all Windows editions as if value 0 always means full diagnostic-data disablement.
tradeoffs: Diagnostic data can be reduced to the lowest documented level on supported editions.

records.name: Preserve Zone Information on Attachments
records.summary: Official Attachment Manager policy for stopping Windows from preserving zone-of-origin metadata on downloaded files. The local Microsoft AttachmentManager.admx file explicitly documents the same Attachments policy key, SaveZoneInformation value name, and enabled/disabled values that the app uses.

decision.why: The local Microsoft AttachmentManager.admx file explicitly defines SaveZoneInformation under Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\Attachments with enabledValue 1 and disabledValue 2, and the paired ADML help text states that enabling the policy stops Windows from marking attachments with zone information while disabled or not configured keeps marking enabled. The app writes that documented enabled-policy state.

app_current_implementation.notes: The current app writes SaveZoneInformation = 1, which matches the documented policy state that stops Windows from preserving zone information on attachments.

allowed_values.meaning: Windows marks attachments with their zone information.

allowed_values.label: Do not preserve zone information
allowed_values.meaning: Windows does not mark downloaded attachments with zone-of-origin information.

allowed_values.meaning: Windows marks downloaded attachments with zone-of-origin information.

windows_defaults.applies_to: Windows systems supporting Attachment Manager policy

recommended_profiles.bad_when: Mark-of-the-Web tagging is causing a specific compatibility problem you accept the risk of bypassing.
tradeoffs: Downloaded files keep their origin metadata.
Windows can continue making risk assessments based on origin.

avoid_for: General users

recommended_profiles.bad_when: You rely on Windows to apply warnings and protections based on file origin.
tradeoffs: MOTW-style zone tagging is removed.

records.name: Disable NTFS Encryption (EFS)
records.summary: Validated record with machine-checkable proof for the official NTFS encryption policy surface. The app now writes only the documented policy path.

decision.why: The official EFS policy surface is now validated and the app writes only the documented policy path. The setting remains high-impact and is not recommended for general users because it disables EFS on NTFS volumes.

records.name: Delivery Optimization Download Mode
records.summary: Disables Delivery Optimization peer-to-peer update sharing by writing DODownloadMode = 0 under HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\DeliveryOptimization. The local Microsoft DeliveryOptimization ADMX and ADML files define value 0 as HTTP only with no peering. The app previously wrote the same value under HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\PolicyManager\default\DeliveryOptimization; it now uses the documented policy path.

decision.why: The official Delivery Optimization policy path and enum values are clear from the local Microsoft ADMX and ADML files, and the app now writes the documented policy surface directly.

app_current_implementation.notes: The app now writes the official HTTP-only value 0 under HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\DeliveryOptimization\DODownloadMode.

allowed_values.meaning: Delivery Optimization uses HTTP blended with peering behind the same NAT.

allowed_values.label: Group peering
allowed_values.meaning: Delivery Optimization uses HTTP blended with peering across a private group.

allowed_values.label: Internet peering
allowed_values.meaning: Delivery Optimization uses HTTP blended with Internet peering.

allowed_values.label: Simple
allowed_values.meaning: HTTP only, no peering, and no use of the Delivery Optimization cloud service.

allowed_values.label: Bypass
allowed_values.meaning: Bypass Delivery Optimization.

records.name: Password Reveal Button
records.summary: Legacy validated record without machine-checkable validation proof. Policy-backed control for the password reveal button in Windows credential entry surfaces. Turning it off slightly reduces shoulder-surfing convenience risks, but users lose the built-in way to briefly reveal what they typed.

decision.why: The local Microsoft CredUI policy files explicitly define the policy path Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\CredUI, the value name DisablePasswordReveal, and the enabled/disabled mapping. The app writes the documented enabled-state value 1 on that exact policy surface.

app_current_implementation.notes: The current app writes DisablePasswordReveal = 1 under the machine policy path, which matches the documented enabled policy state for hiding the button.

allowed_values.meaning: Windows keeps the normal password reveal button behavior.

avoid_for: Shared or higher-sensitivity machines that want fewer reveal affordances

recommended_profiles.bad_when: You want to remove the reveal button everywhere this policy applies.

avoid_for: Users who rely on the reveal button to reduce typing mistakes

recommended_profiles.bad_when: Users regularly need visual confirmation while typing passwords.

records.name: Exploit Protection System Mitigations
records.summary: Microsoft documents exploit protection system mitigations through the Windows Security app, the ProcessMitigation PowerShell cmdlets, and an Exploit Guard policy that points to an XML configuration file. The app now imports the documented XML policy surface instead of writing raw MitigationOptions and MitigationAuditOptions blobs directly under the kernel registry path.

decision.why: Microsoft's documented exploit protection surface is XML, Windows Security, and the ProcessMitigation cmdlets. The app now imports the documented XML baseline and restores from a captured backup XML via the same command surface. The tweak remains risky for general users because exploit protection changes can affect application compatibility.

app_current_implementation.notes: The app now stages the checked-in Exploit Protection XML baseline to %TEMP%\WindowsOptimizer\ExploitProtection\security-disable-system-mitigations.xml and imports it via Set-ProcessMitigation -PolicyFilePath. Rollback restores the exported backup XML via the same documented import path. The raw MitigationOptions and MitigationAuditOptions kernel blobs were removed from the provider.

writes.notes: The app stages the checked-in XML resource to a temp file and imports it through the documented ProcessMitigation surface.

allowed_values.label: Not configured
allowed_values.meaning: Common exploit protection settings are not forced by this Group Policy setting, and local system settings remain in effect.

allowed_values.label: Use common exploit protection XML
allowed_values.meaning: The policy is enabled and points to an XML file that defines the common exploit protection settings to apply.

windows_defaults.applies_to: Windows systems using local exploit protection defaults or explicitly configured Windows Security settings

states.rationale: Leave the common Exploit Guard XML policy unset unless a managed mitigation baseline is intentionally deployed.

intended_for: Systems that should keep Microsoft's normal exploit protection baseline
avoid_for: Teams that intentionally deploy a reviewed exploit protection XML baseline

recommended_profiles.bad_when: You need a fully researched and tested custom exploit protection baseline.
tradeoffs: Windows keeps the documented exploit protection management flow.

avoid_for: General users

recommended_profiles.good_when: You are documenting what the app currently writes.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You need a fully documented and officially mapped exploit protection configuration.
tradeoffs: The app's XML-backed exploit protection baseline is preserved for research.

evidence.title: Local Microsoft ExploitGuard.admx mapping
evidence.summary: The local ADMX file maps the Exploit Protection policy under HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows Defender ExploitGuard\Exploit Protection and stores the configured XML path in ExploitProtectionSettings.

evidence.title: Local Microsoft ExploitGuard.adml help text
evidence.summary: The local ADML help text says administrators should configure a device's system and application mitigation settings, export them as XML, and then point the policy to that XML file.

evidence.title: Microsoft Learn exploit protection guidance
evidence.summary: Microsoft documents exploit protection through Windows Security, ProcessMitigation PowerShell commands, and exported XML configuration, and warns that some mitigation technologies can have compatibility issues and should be audited before broad deployment.

evidence.title: Current app implementation
evidence.summary: The provider writes raw binary MitigationOptions and MitigationAuditOptions values directly under HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Control\Session Manager\kernel.

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: Get-ProcessMitigation -RegistryConfigFilePath filename.xml; Set-ProcessMitigation -PolicyFilePath filename.xml; ExploitGuard.admx policy key: HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows Defender ExploitGuard\Exploit Protection, value ExploitProtectionSettings
validation_proof.notes: Local official Microsoft ADMX/ADML files and Microsoft Learn document Exploit Protection through the XML import/export surface. Guest validation confirmed that a raw export, an imported XML policy file, and the restored export all round-trip cleanly when the default Fonts Audit="false" normalization is ignored. The app now uses the documented XML import path instead of raw kernel mitigation blobs.

records.name: Windows Update Driver Search Policy
records.summary: Legacy validated record without machine-checkable validation proof. Policy-backed control for stopping Windows Update from being used as a device-driver source and for excluding driver-classified updates from Windows quality updates. This can reduce unexpected driver changes, but it can also block useful driver delivery when no local or vendor-provided driver is available.

decision.why: The local Microsoft DeviceSetup, ICM, and WindowsUpdate policy files explicitly define SearchOrderConfig, DontSearchWindowsUpdate, and ExcludeWUDriversInQualityUpdate on the exact policy paths used by the app. The app writes the documented values that keep Windows Update out of driver sourcing and quality-update driver delivery.

app_current_implementation.notes: The current app writes SearchOrderConfig = 0, DontSearchWindowsUpdate = 1, and ExcludeWUDriversInQualityUpdate = 1. Together these values align with the documented direction of keeping Windows Update out of the driver's sourcing and quality-update path.

allowed_values.label: Not configured
allowed_values.meaning: Windows keeps the normal driver-source search behavior without this explicit policy override.

allowed_values.label: Do not search Windows Update
allowed_values.meaning: If this policy is enabled, Windows does not search Windows Update for device drivers.

allowed_values.label: Always search Windows Update
allowed_values.meaning: If this policy is enabled, Windows always searches Windows Update for device drivers.

allowed_values.label: Search Windows Update only if needed
allowed_values.meaning: If this policy is enabled, Windows searches Windows Update for device drivers only when needed.

allowed_values.label: Not configured
allowed_values.meaning: Windows keeps the normal driver-search behavior without this explicit security option override.

allowed_values.label: Windows Update search allowed
allowed_values.meaning: If the policy is disabled, Windows Update can be searched for drivers when no local drivers are present.

allowed_values.label: Windows Update search blocked
allowed_values.meaning: If the policy is enabled, Windows Update is not searched for drivers when no local drivers are present.

allowed_values.label: Windows includes driver-classified updates
allowed_values.meaning: Windows Update can include updates that have a Driver classification.

allowed_values.label: Do not exclude driver-classified quality updates
allowed_values.meaning: The policy is disabled and Windows Update can include driver-classified updates.

allowed_values.label: Exclude driver-classified quality updates
allowed_values.meaning: Windows Update does not include driver-classified updates in Windows quality updates.

windows_defaults.applies_to: Windows systems supporting the DriverSearching policies

Leave the Windows Update driver-search block unset.
Leave the driver-exclusion quality-update policy unset.

avoid_for: Machines that must avoid Windows Update supplied drivers

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: DeviceSetup.admx: <policy name="DriverSearchPlaces_SearchOrderConfiguration" ... key="Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\DriverSearching"> ... <enum ... valueName="SearchOrderConfig"> ... <decimal value="0"/>; ICM.admx: key="Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\DriverSearching" valueName="DontSearchWindowsUpdate"; WindowsUpdate.admx: key="Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\WindowsUpdate" valueName="ExcludeWUDriversInQualityUpdate"> <enabledValue><decimal value="1" /></enabledValue>.
validation_proof.notes: The local official Microsoft policy files define the exact DriverSearching and WindowsUpdate policy paths used by the app. SearchOrderConfig = 0 means do not search Windows Update, DontSearchWindowsUpdate = 1 blocks Windows Update device-driver searching, and ExcludeWUDriversInQualityUpdate = 1 excludes driver-classified updates from Windows quality updates.

records.name: Windows Sudo Mode Policy
records.summary: Legacy validated record without machine-checkable validation proof. Official policy-backed control for Windows sudo behavior. The current app sets the maximum allowed sudo mode to Normal, which allows in-place use and also permits the stricter modes.

decision.why: The local Microsoft Sudo policy files explicitly define the policy path HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Sudo, the value name Enabled, and enum values 0 through 3. The app writes the documented Normal mode value 3 on that exact machine policy surface.

app_current_implementation.notes: The current app writes Enabled = 3, which matches the documented Normal mode in the official Sudo policy.

allowed_values.meaning: sudo.exe is disabled on the machine.

allowed_values.label: Force new window
allowed_values.meaning: sudo can run only by launching the elevated app in a new console window.

allowed_values.label: Disable input
allowed_values.meaning: sudo can run in the current console without interactive input, and also allows the Force new window mode.

allowed_values.label: Normal
allowed_values.meaning: sudo can run in the current console normally, and also allows the stricter Force new window and Disable input modes.

records.name: Windows PowerShell Script Execution Policy
records.summary: Sets the official Windows PowerShell Group Policy execution surface to allow all scripts by writing EnableScripts = 1 and ExecutionPolicy = Unrestricted under HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\PowerShell. The app previously wrote only the legacy shell preference value under HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\PowerShell\1\ShellIds\Microsoft.PowerShell; it now writes the documented Group Policy path instead.

decision.why: The official PowerShell Group Policy surface is clear, and the app now writes both required policy values. The record remains non-publishable for general apply because Unrestricted materially weakens script-execution protections.

app_current_implementation.notes: The app now writes both official PowerShell Group Policy values through one batch tweak: EnableScripts = 1 and ExecutionPolicy = Unrestricted under HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\PowerShell.

records.name: User Account Control Prompting Profile
records.summary: Official UAC registry values for the least restrictive administrator prompt behavior while User Account Control remains enabled. Microsoft documents the same path, value names, defaults, and numeric meanings that the app writes, and a Win25H2Clean Procmon capture on 2026-03-21 confirmed the UAC settings surface reads those same values.

decision.why: Microsoft explicitly documents the UAC registry path, default values, and numeric meanings for EnableLUA, ConsentPromptBehaviorAdmin, and PromptOnSecureDesktop under HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\System, and the app writes that exact control surface. A Win25H2Clean Procmon capture on 2026-03-21 confirmed the UAC settings surface reads those same values. This is still not a casual-safe profile, so the record remains non-default even though validation is complete.

records.name: Automatic Restart on System Failure

records.name: Restart Apps After Sign-In
records.area: Feature Setting vs Observed Registry Setting
records.summary: Microsoft documents the Restart apps sign-in feature, and a guest-side reversible probe on Win25H2Clean confirmed the raw RestartApps registry mapping under HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon. records.name: Game Mode
records.summary: The current app writes HKCU\Software\Microsoft\GameBar\AutoGameModeEnabled = 1. Guest-side Procmon captures on 2026-03-20 against the interactive Administrator profile confirmed SystemSettings.exe queries AutoGameModeEnabled with Data:1 and Data:0 in separate reversible captures. evidence.title: Microsoft Learn: Introduction to threaded DPCs
evidence.summary: Microsoft documents HKLM\System\CCS\Control\SessionManager\Kernel\ThreadDpcEnable, says threaded DPCs are enabled by default, and states that setting the value to 0 disables them and makes them execute as ordinary DPCs.

evidence.title: Current app implementation
evidence.summary: The provider writes ThreadDpcEnable = 1 for the current tweak.

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: A threaded DPC is a DPC that the system executes at IRQL equal to PASSIVE_LEVEL. Threaded DPCs are enabled by default, but you can disable them by setting the HKLM\System\CCS\Control\SessionManager\Kernel\ThreadDpcEnable registry key to zero. When threaded DPCs are disabled, they execute as ordinary DPCs.
validation_proof.notes: The Microsoft Learn page gives the exact registry path and documents that the feature is enabled by default and disabled by setting ThreadDpcEnable to 0. The app's value 1 is treated as an explicit enabled-state reset on that documented surface.

The DefaultValue can have the following values: 0: Enables 8dot3 name creation for all volumes on the system. 1: Disables 8dot3 name creation for all volumes on the system. 2: Sets 8dot3 name creation on a per volume basis. 3: Disables 8dot3 name creation for all volumes except the system volume.

records.name: Shutdown Timeouts Bundle

Microsoft documents the shutdown-timeout concepts and the supporting Microsoft Q&A threads now show the user-side registry values, so the bundle can be validated as observed implementation rather than left as an open research gap.

decision.why: Microsoft documents the timeout concepts and the supporting Microsoft Q&A threads now show the user-side registry values for the bundle. The record is validated as observed implementation, not as a recommendation for general users.
