recommended_profiles.bad_when: You need an officially documented registry mapping before applying changes.
tradeoffs: The language bar is hidden for the current user.
The mapping is repo-backed in this review, not primary-source-backed.

evidence.title: Microsoft Q&A: language bar ShowStatus hidden-state example
evidence.summary: A Microsoft Q&A thread shows ShowStatus = 3 for the CTF language bar hidden state.

evidence.title: Repo visibility notes for language bar
evidence.summary: The repo documents ShowStatus values for HKCU\Software\Microsoft\CTF\LangBar as 0 = Floating on desktop, 3 = Hidden, and 4 = Docked in the taskbar.

evidence.summary: The provider writes ShowStatus = 3 under HKCU\Software\Microsoft\CTF\LangBar for the current tweak.

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: HKCU\Software\Microsoft\CTF\LangBar\ShowStatus = DWORD 3
validation_proof.notes: Validated against a Microsoft Q&A thread that explicitly shows ShowStatus = 3 for the hidden language-bar state.

records.name: Start Menu Most Used List
records.summary: Policy for the Start menu Most used list. The local Microsoft StartMenu.admx file documents the same Explorer policy key and ShowOrHideMostUsedApps enum value used by the app.

decision.why: The local Microsoft StartMenu.admx file explicitly defines the ShowOrHideMostUsedApps enum policy under Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Explorer and the app writes the documented force-hide value of 2.

app_current_implementation.notes: The current app writes ShowOrHideMostUsedApps = 2, which matches the documented force-hide state.

allowed_values.label: Not configured
allowed_values.meaning: The policy does not force the Most used list, so the user can control it in Settings.

allowed_values.label: Policy set to not configured behavior
allowed_values.meaning: The enum explicitly selects the not-configured behavior, which still leaves the Most used list under user control.

allowed_values.label: Force show Most used
allowed_values.meaning: The policy forces the Most used list to stay shown and the user cannot hide it in Settings.

allowed_values.label: Force hide Most used
allowed_values.meaning: The policy forces the Most used list to stay hidden and the user cannot show it in Settings.

windows_defaults.label: Windows machine-policy default
windows_defaults.applies_to: Windows 10 21H2 and newer systems that support the Start menu policy

states.rationale: The policy is not configured by default.

recommended_profiles.label: Windows default
intended_for: Users who want Start behavior to stay personalizable
avoid_for: Shared devices where usage patterns should stay hidden

states.rationale: Do not force the Most used list either way.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want users to decide whether Start shows Most used apps.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You want the list hidden on every sign-in profile using the device.
tradeoffs: Users keep control from Settings.
The Start menu may still show the Most used list.

recommended_profiles.label: Cleaner Start menu
intended_for: Users who want less visual clutter in Start
avoid_for: Users who rely on Most used as a launcher

states.rationale: Force the Most used list hidden through policy.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want Start to stay cleaner and show less usage history.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You want quick access to frequently used apps from Start.
tradeoffs: The Most used list is hidden and the user cannot turn it back on in Settings.
A convenient launcher area disappears for users who like it.

evidence.title: Local Microsoft StartMenu.admx mapping
evidence.summary: The local ADMX file defines ShowOrHideMostUsedApps as an enum policy with values 0, 1, and 2 under the Explorer policy key.

evidence.title: Local Microsoft StartMenu.adml help text
evidence.summary: The local ADML help text explains that Show forces the Most used list visible, Hide forces it hidden, and disabled, not configured, or enum value 0 all leave the behavior under user control.

evidence.title: Current app implementation
evidence.summary: The provider writes ShowOrHideMostUsedApps = 2 for the current tweak.

evidence.title: Existing tweak provenance record
evidence.summary: The repo already tracks provenance for this tweak in the current catalog output.

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: <policy name="ShowOrHideMostUsedApps" class="Both" ... key="Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Explorer"> ... <enum id="ShowOrHideMostUsedAppsDropdown" valueName="ShowOrHideMostUsedApps" required="true">
validation_proof.notes: The local official Microsoft ADMX file defines the same Explorer policy key and ShowOrHideMostUsedApps enum surface used by the app. The paired ADML help text documents the force-show, force-hide, and user-control behavior for the enum values.

records.name: People Bar on the Taskbar
records.summary: User policy for the People Bar taskbar feature. Good for a cleaner taskbar, but it removes the My People experience and related pinning UI.

decision.why: Local Microsoft StartMenu policy files document the exact user-policy surface, allowed values, and behavior for HidePeopleBar. The app writes the documented hide value.

app_current_implementation.notes: The current app writes HidePeopleBar = 1 for the current user, which matches the documented hide state.

allowed_values.label: People Bar visible
allowed_values.meaning: The policy is disabled and the People Bar is not hidden.

allowed_values.label: People Bar hidden
allowed_values.meaning: The policy is enabled and the People Bar is removed from the taskbar.

windows_defaults.label: Windows user-policy default
windows_defaults.applies_to: Windows 10 version 1709 and later systems that still expose the People Bar policy

states.rationale: Microsoft documents the default value as 0, meaning the feature is not hidden.

recommended_profiles.label: Windows default
intended_for: Users who still want the My People feature
avoid_for: Users who want a cleaner taskbar

states.rationale: Keep the feature visible.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want the People Bar to remain available.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You do not want this taskbar feature at all.
tradeoffs: The My People experience remains available.
Taskbar UI remains less minimal.

recommended_profiles.label: Clean taskbar
intended_for: Users who prefer fewer taskbar items
avoid_for: Users who use My People or contact pinning

states.rationale: Hide the People Bar and remove the related UI.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want the taskbar to stay simpler and more focused.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You want quick contact access from the taskbar.
tradeoffs: The People icon disappears from the taskbar.
Users cannot pin people to the taskbar.

evidence.summary: The repo already tracks provenance for this tweak in the current catalog output.

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: <policy name="HidePeopleBar" class="User" ... key="Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Explorer" valueName="HidePeopleBar">; StartMenu.adml: This policy allows you to remove the People Bar from the taskbar and disables the My People experience.
validation_proof.notes: Local official ADMX defines the exact user-policy key and value name; local ADML confirms that enabling the policy removes the People Bar and disables the My People experience.

records.name: Classic Context Menu on Windows 11
records.summary: The current app uses the known Windows 11 classic-context-menu CLSID workaround by creating HKCU\Software\Classes\CLSID\{86ca1aa0-34aa-4e8b-a509-50c905bae2a2}\InprocServer32 with an empty default value. Microsoft Q&A now documents the same registry workaround, so the record is validated.

decision.why: Microsoft Q&A now documents the same CLSID workaround: create the InprocServer32 key and leave the default value blank to restore the classic Windows 11 context menu. The record matches the app and can be published as validated.

app_current_implementation.notes: The app writes an empty default value under the known Windows 11 classic-context-menu CLSID path.

allowed_values.label: Windows 11 default menu
allowed_values.meaning: Repo notes say removing the custom CLSID key reverts to the newer Windows 11 context menu.

allowed_values.label: Classic context menu workaround active
allowed_values.meaning: Repo notes say an empty default value under this CLSID path restores the classic Windows 10 style context menu on Windows 11.

windows_defaults.label: Windows 11 default context menu
windows_defaults.applies_to: Windows 11 systems without the custom CLSID workaround key

states.rationale: Repo notes say deleting the custom key restores the default Windows 11 context menu.

recommended_profiles.label: Windows 11 default
intended_for: People staying on stock Windows 11 context-menu behavior
avoid_for: Users who strongly prefer the classic menu

states.rationale: Stay on the stock Windows 11 menu by not creating the CLSID workaround key.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want the standard Windows 11 context menu behavior.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You want the classic context menu immediately available.
tradeoffs: Windows keeps the newer context menu behavior.
The record avoids publishing an unsourced registry workaround as validated.

recommended_profiles.label: Classic context menu
intended_for: Windows 11 users who prefer the older context menu
Power users who want full right-click commands visible immediately
avoid_for: Users who only want Microsoft-documented settings in the validated set

states.rationale: Use the repo-documented CLSID workaround that the app currently writes.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You explicitly want the classic Windows 10 style context menu on Windows 11.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You need an officially documented Microsoft registry surface before applying changes.
tradeoffs: The classic context menu returns.
The mapping is treated as a known workaround in this review, not a primary-source-backed validated setting.

evidence.title: Repo visibility notes for classic context menu
evidence.summary: The repo documents that creating the CLSID InprocServer32 key restores the Windows 10 style context menu on Windows 11 and that removing the key reverts it.

evidence.title: Repo visibility use-case guide for classic context menu
evidence.summary: The use-case guide repeats the CLSID path and empty default value used for the classic-context-menu workaround.

evidence.title: Current app implementation
evidence.summary: The provider writes an empty default value under the classic-context-menu CLSID path for the current tweak.

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: reg add "HKCU\Software\Classes\CLSID\{86ca1aa0-34aa-4e8b-a509-50c905bae2a2}\InprocServer32" /f /ve
validation_proof.notes: Microsoft Q&A documents the CLSID workaround and the empty default value used to restore the classic context menu.
